title: Let's Objectify Some Plastic - featuring Massive Darkness
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DS_neiUmgrA

Daniel (@dungeondive) launches a new series, "Let's Objectify Some Plastic," built on a cheerful provocation: he's not really a miniatures person — he'd happily snap his fingers and turn every plastic figure into a cardboard standee with good art — and he argues that in most board games the 3D qualities of a mini (facing, positioning) simply don't matter. Their real job is to look cool and grab attention on Kickstarter.

So the conceit here is to build a Massive Darkness party purely on the strength of the sculpts. Working through the Quest of Crystal and Lava heroes knowing nothing of their names or classes, he judges each on looks alone — admiring a Dark Souls-armoured warrior, a burly Conan-like barbarian with a warhammer, a menacing witch-doctor woman — and skewering the odd off-balance "Massive Darkness lean" of several female poses. He settles on two heroes (revealed to be Miriam, a battle wizard, and the bonecrusher "little Ned") and then assembles a custom wandering-monster deck of only the figures he likes: trolls, a magnificent minotaur, cyclopes and cleaver-wielding giants.

It's a playful, opinionated tour of CMON's plastic rather than a rules breakdown — the actual playthrough and review, he promises, will follow off-camera.

----

Do miniatures genuinely add to your games, or would you take Daniel's deal and swap them all for great cardboard standees tomorrow?
